There is a long tradition of research going back to the 1950s on the ability of gifts and rewards to influence and persuade people. But gifts and rewards are not the same. And one topic that's been studied extensively is which one is more effective? To find out, researchers mailed a survey to construction company managers. The message included with the survey estimated that it would take a full hour to complete. Not a short survey. The researchers randomly assigned managers to one of two conditions. In the first condition, managers would get $50 if they completed the full survey. Not a bad payout for an hour of paperwork. This was the reward group, since they got the money only after they completed the task. The second group of managers was made a different offer. Instead of a promise to pay for a completed survey, these managers received a $5 check along with the survey. They were told that the check was theirs to keep, whether or not they completed the survey. No strings attached. This was the gift group, since they got the money before even being asked to complete the task. In the condition where managers were promised $50 as a reward for filling out this long survey, 23% of them completed the task. On the other hand, when managers were given a $5 check as a gift upfront, 52% of them completed the task. A vast improvement over response rates for less total costs. This is the case, because it was found that to get the same number of respondents in the reward group as in the gift group, the sponsors of the survey in the reward approach would have to spend eight times more money. Now you might reasonably worry that many people who received the $5 check in advance would simply cash it without completing the survey. But the researchers found that very few people actually did this. Gifts were just more effective than rewards. If they received the $5 check first, they then completed the task. This is such an important feature of the rule of reciprocity, the importance of going first, giving first obligates people to give in return. Even when the payoff is not as large as it might be.
